blocks.content: Dinner at the long table inside, windows open to the sound of the water, candles that Sophie found in a drawer and lit without anyone asking her to. The food was good. The conversation moved the way it did with people who had years of material between them — broad, associative, full of references that required no explanation.

blocks.speaker: Dean
blocks.content: Do you remember the camping trip junior year. When Cade convinced us the trail was a loop.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: It looked like a loop on the map.

blocks.speaker: Marcus
blocks.content: You had the map upside down, Cade.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: I have since developed a healthy respect for compass direction.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: And we were stronger for it.

blocks.speaker: Marcus
blocks.content: I had blisters for a week.

Nadia laughed with them. She had been on that trip. She remembered the blisters and the argument and Marcus carrying Wren's bag the last three miles because her shoulder was bothering her and nobody asking him to. She remembered feeling — back then, before everything — that these were her people. That she had found something real.
She still believed they were her people. She just also believed that what she was doing this weekend was something her people would understand, eventually, if they knew the whole story.

blocks.speaker: Nadia
blocks.content: Sorry. Just — it's nice. Being here. All of us. I was just taking it in.
Marcus raised his glass toward her across the table.
Nadia raised her glass and looked at Marcus and thought: tomorrow.
